perturbation.original_text: Before a user can gain access to the website, they must first verify their birthday (users must be 21 and over to even view the website).
perturbation.changed_text: Before a user can gain access to the website, they must first verify their age (users must be 18 and over to even view the website).
perturbation.explanation: This changes the age verification requirement from 21 to 18, which is a direct contradiction of Washington State law regarding cannabis use.
perturbation.law_explanation: Washington State law (RCW 69.50.4014) clearly states that individuals must be 21 years of age or older to purchase, possess, or consume cannabis. By stating users must be 18 or over, this contract directly contradicts the state law, making the age verification process non-compliant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 69.50.4014: Possession of forty grams or less of cannabis—Penalty—Referral to assessment and services. Print RCWs > Title 69 > Chapter 69.50 > Section 69.50.4014 69.50.4013 << 69.50.4014 >> 69.50.4015 PDF RCW 69.50.4014 Possession of forty grams or less of cannabis — Penalty — Referral to assessment and services. (1) Except as provided in RCW 69.50.401 (2)(c) or as otherwise authorized by this chapter, any person found guilty of knowing possession of 40 grams or less of cannabis is guilty of a misdemeanor. The prosecutor is encouraged to divert cases under this section for assessment, treatment, or other services. (2) In lieu of jail booking and referral to the prosecutor, law enforcement is encouraged to offer a referral to assessment and services available under RCW 10.31.110 or other program or entity responsible for receiving referrals in lieu of legal system involvement, which may include, but are not limited to, arrest and jail alternative programs established under RCW 36.28A.450 , law enforcement assisted diversion programs established under RCW 71.24.589 , and the recovery navigator program established under RCW 71.24.115 . [ 2023 sp.s. c 1 s 3 ; 2022 c 16 s 88 ; (2022 c 16 s 87 expired July 1, 2023); (2021 c 311 s 10 expired July 1, 2023); 2015 2nd sp.s. c 4 s 505 ; 2003 c 53 s 335 .] NOTES: Effective date — 2023 sp.s. c 1 ss 1-5, 7-11, and 41: See note following RCW 69.50.4011 . Effective date — 2022 c 16 ss 5, 9, 86, and 88: See note following RCW 69.50.4013 . Expiration date — 2022 c 16 ss 4, 8, 85, and 87: See note following RCW 69.50.4013 . Expiration date — 2021 c 311 ss 8-10 and 12: See note following RCW 69.50.4011 . Findings — Intent — Effective dates — 2015 2nd sp.s. c 4: See notes following RCW 69.50.334 . Intent — Effective date — 2003 c 53: See notes following RCW 2.48.180 .

perturbation.justification: The changed text lowers the age requirement to 18, while the law requires users to be 21 or older to possess cannabis. This is a direct contradiction. Although the provided snippets do not explicitly state the age requirement for cannabis use, the Law Explanation clearly states that "Washington State law (RCW 69.50.4014) clearly states that individuals must be 21 years of age or older to purchase, possess, or consume cannabis." The change, therefore, creates a contradiction by setting a lower age limit than the state law allows for cannabis use.

perturbation.original_text: Users will pay by linking their bank accounts via Evergreen Gateway o Account information will be stored and used for future orders
perturbation.changed_text: Users will pay by linking their bank accounts via Evergreen Gateway o Account information may be stored or not, at the discretion of Pelican Delivers, and used for future orders
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces uncertainty about the storage of payment information. While the original contract suggests storage, this modification makes it discretionary, potentially conflicting with data security and privacy laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Washington Consumer Protection Act (RCW 19.86) generally prohibits unfair or deceptive practices. Failing to disclose or being ambiguous about the storage of financial data or changing the policy arbitrarily without notice could be construed as deceptive, especially if it leads to unauthorized charges or data breaches. Consumers have a right to know if and how their financial data is stored.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Consumer Protection | Washington State Consumer Protection Contact: Email Consumer Resource Center Available Monday - Friday, 10 a.m. - 3 p.m. 1-800-551-4636 (Washington Only) 206-464-6684 File a consumer complaint File an online complaint here Mail paper complaint forms to: Attorney General’s Office Consumer Resource Center 800 Fifth Avenue, Suite 2000 Seattle, WA 98104 Overview The Consumer Protection Division is composed of attorneys and professional staff. The division enforces the Consumer Protection Act ( RCW 19.86 ) and other statutes to help keep the Washington marketplace free of unfair and deceptive practices. The division investigates and files legal actions to stop unfair and deceptive practices, recovers refunds for consumers, seeks penalties against offending entities, and recovers costs and fees to ensure that wrongdoers pay for their actions. Division staff participate in the many legislative initiatives that affect consumer protection each year. The division’s Consumer Resource Center provides an informal complaint resolution service. The informal complaint resolution process includes notifying businesses of written complaints and facilitating communication between the consumer and the business to assist in resolving the complaint. The Consumer Protection Division provides information and education to businesses and the public on consumer issues and issues alerts and press releases to warn consumers and businesses about fraudulent or predatory activities. The Consumer Protection Division is also responsible for the administration of Washington’s Lemon Law for new motor vehicle warranty enforcement. The Lemon Law program provides important information to consumers regarding their rights and remedies under the Lemon Law and the Lemon Law arbitration process. The Lemon Law program also provides consumer and industry education and enforcement regarding manufacturer and dealer obligations under the law. The division also administers the Manufactured/Mobile Home Dispute Resolution Program (MHDRP). The MHDRP facilitates dispute resolution between manufactured/mobile home landlords and tenants in situations where the tenant owns the home but rents a space from the landlord. The MHDRP is authorized to investigate, issue findings and impose fines if there has been a violation of the law. The MHDRP produces and distributes educational materials regarding the Manufactured/Mobile Home Landlord-Tenant Act (MHLTA). Back to top Legal Services Provided (to the state) The division r
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the storage of users' financial data. While the original text implied storage, the changed text makes it discretionary, which creates uncertainty that could be construed as deceptive under the Washington Consumer Protection Act (RCW 19.86), as stated in the Law Explanation. Snippet 2 mentions that the Consumer Protection Division enforces the Consumer Protection Act (RCW 19.86) and investigates unfair or deceptive practices. Making the storage of financial data discretionary could be seen as an unfair or deceptive practice because consumers have a right to know if and how their financial data is stored, and changing the policy without notice could lead to unauthorized charges or data breaches, according to the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Commission fees - 25% commission on delivery orders. Admin will have the ability to change the % or fee based on dispensary
perturbation.changed_text: Commission fees - 25% commission on delivery orders. Admin will have the ability to change the % or fee based on dispensary, without any prior notice.
perturbation.explanation: This addition of 'without any prior notice' can contradict fair business practice laws. Sudden changes to commission fees without notice could violate principles of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Washington Uniform Commercial Code - Duty of Good Faith (RCW 62A.1-304)
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 62A.1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance or enforcement of every contract or duty within the UCC. Changing commission fees without prior notice could be considered a violation of good faith, particularly if it causes financial harm to the dispensaries who rely on a predictable commission structure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 62A.1-304: Obligation of good faith. Print RCWs > Title 62A > Chapter 62A.1 > Section 62A.1-304 62A.1-303 << 62A.1-304 >> 62A.1-305 PDF RCW 62A.1-304 Obligation of good faith. Every contract or duty within this title imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. [ 2012 c 214 s 118 .] NOTES: Application — Savings — 2012 c 214: See notes following RCW 62A.1-101 .
perturbation.justification: The addition of 'without any prior notice' directly contradicts the obligation of good faith as outlined in RCW 62A.1-304. The original text allowed for changes to commission fees, but the amended text removes the requirement for any notice, potentially causing financial harm and undermining the principle of fair dealing. RCW 62A.1-304 states, 'Every contract or duty within this title imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Changing the commission without notice could be interpreted as acting in bad faith, therefore a legal contradiction is created.
